query: How well does this ACT policy generalize across manipulated-object properties, and where is its first likely weakness?

proposal.hypothesis: Reducing only the block geometry to 0.8x will restore hammer-block contact and official success; if it does not, the observed 1.2x failure is unlikely to be caused solely by scale-induced contact clearance.

requested_perturbation.description: Decrease only the target block geometry scale to 0.8x, keeping the hammer, placements, task procedure, and ACT checkpoint unchanged.
controlled_changes: target block geometry scale set to 0.8x

task_need.description: TaskGen must force-generate a scene variant with only the block geometry scaled to 0.8x and preserve all other task elements.

tool_need.description: ToolGen must retrieve or generate an observable reporting hammer-block contact occurrence and peak contact impulse, minimum XY distance, and official success for the rollout.

proposal.rationale: The 1.2x scale test failed without physical contact, but a single direction cannot distinguish a scale-induced clearance problem from a broader ACT weakness on altered geometry. Testing the opposite bounded scale direction is the most informative causal discriminator available: recovery would indicate a directional contact-clearance sensitivity, while persistent no-contact failure would identify geometry-scale generalization as an early weakness independent of direction.
